questions.q: Which property distinguishes $\mathbb{R}$ from $\mathbb{Q}$?
choices: Every nonempty bounded-above subset of $\mathbb{R}$ has a least upper bound
questions.hint: $\mathbb{Q}$ is an ordered, Archimedean field, and closed under addition.
questions.explain: $\mathbb{Q}$ is an ordered, Archimedean field, and closed under addition. The distinguishing property is the least-upper-bound (Dedekind) completeness: the set $\{q\in\mathbb{Q}:q^2<2\}$ is bounded above but has no supremum in $\mathbb{Q}$.

questions.q: Let $S = \{1 - 1/n : n\in\mathbb{N}_{\ge 1}\}\subset\mathbb{R}$. What is $\sup S$?
questions.hint: $S$ is bounded above by $1$ and its elements approach $1$.
questions.explain: $S$ is bounded above by $1$ and its elements approach $1$. Since $1$ is an upper bound and no smaller number is, $\sup S = 1$. Note $1\notin S$ — the supremum need not be attained.

questions.q: Which statement is equivalent to $\mathbb{R}$ being Cauchy-complete?
choices: Every sequence has a convergent subsequence
questions.hint: Cauchy completeness is exactly the statement that every Cauchy sequence has a limit in the space.
questions.explain: Cauchy completeness is exactly the statement that every Cauchy sequence has a limit in the space. Bounded sequences need not converge ($(-1)^n$); monotone-bounded sequences do, and bounded sequences have convergent subsequences (Bolzano–Weierstrass) — but those are consequences, not definitions.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps to prove that every Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ converges?
choices: Show it is bounded → extract a convergent subsequence (B–W) → show the full sequence converges to the same limit
Show it converges → conclude it is bounded → apply the least-upper-bound property
Apply the Archimedean property directly to get the limit → verify the Cauchy condition
Extract a monotone subsequence → apply the LUB property → ignore the rest of the sequence
hard.explain: Proof chain: (1) Every Cauchy sequence is bounded. (2) Bolzano–Weierstrass extracts a convergent subsequence $a_{n_k}\to L$. (3) For any $\varepsilon>0$ choose $N$ so $|a_m-a_n|<\varepsilon/2$ for $m,n>N$ and $k$ with $n_k>N$ and $|a_{n_k}-L|<\varepsilon/2$; then $|a_n-L|\le|a_n-a_{n_k}|+|a_{n_k}-L|<\varepsilon$.

hard.q: Let $A = \{x\in\mathbb{Q} : x^2 < 3\}$. In $\mathbb{R}$, $\sup A = \sqrt{3}$. How many rational numbers satisfy $x^2 = 3$?
hard.explain: $\sqrt{3}$ is irrational (its square is $3$, but $3$ is not a perfect square, and a standard argument shows $\sqrt{3}\notin\mathbb{Q}$). So there are $0$ rational solutions to $x^2=3$, illustrating why $\sup A$ is not attained in $\mathbb{Q}$.

questions.q: If $f\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous with $f(0)=-1$ and $f(1)=2$, which theorem guarantees a root of $f$?
questions.hint: IVT: a continuous function on $[a,b]$ takes every value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$.
questions.explain: IVT: a continuous function on $[a,b]$ takes every value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$. Since $0$ lies between $-1$ and $2$, some $c\in[0,1]$ has $f(c)=0$.

questions.q: For $f(x)=2x+3$ and $a=1$, with $\varepsilon=0.01$, what is the largest $\delta$ that works in the $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ definition of continuity?
Which of these is NOT guaranteed for a continuous $f\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$?
questions.hint: Continuity on a closed bounded interval gives EVT (attains max/min), uniform continuity (via compactness), and that the image is compact and connected (so a closed bounded interval).
questions.explain: Continuity on a closed bounded interval gives EVT (attains max/min), uniform continuity (via compactness), and that the image is compact and connected (so a closed bounded interval). Differentiability is a strictly stronger condition — e.g., $f(x)=|x-1/2|$ is continuous on $[0,1]$ but not differentiable at $1/2$.

hard.q: Arrange the steps of the proof that every Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ converges.
items: Show that the Cauchy sequence $(a_n)$ is bounded (take $\varepsilon=1$ in the Cauchy condition and use finitely many initial terms to bound the rest).
Apply the Bolzano–Weierstrass theorem to extract a convergent subsequence $a_{n_k}\to L$.

Use the Cauchy condition once more: given $\varepsilon>0$, pick $N$ with $|a_m-a_n|<\varepsilon/2$ for $m,n\ge N$, and pick $k$ with $n_k\ge N$ and $|a_{n_k}-L|<\varepsilon/2$; conclude $|a_n-L|<\varepsilon$ for all $n\ge N$, so the whole sequence converges to $L$.
hard.explain: Boundedness gives B–W a compact interval to work in; B–W produces a convergent subsequence; the Cauchy condition then promotes subsequential convergence to convergence of the whole sequence. Each step uses the previous one's conclusion, so the order is forced.

hard.q: Let $f(x)=x^2\sin(1/x)$ for $x\ne 0$ and $f(0)=0$. Using the $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ definition with $\varepsilon=10^{-4}$, what is the largest $\delta$ guaranteeing $|f(x)-f(0)|<\varepsilon$ for $|x|<\delta$? (Hint: $|f(x)|\le x^2$.)
hard.explain: $|f(x)-0|=|x^2\sin(1/x)|\le x^2 < \varepsilon$ whenever $|x|<\sqrt{\varepsilon}=\sqrt{10^{-4}}=0.01$. So $\delta=0.01$.

hard.q: You are proving the intermediate value theorem: if $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous with $f(a)<0<f(b)$, then some $c\in(a,b)$ has $f(c)=0$. The steps so far use the nested-interval bisection idea.
steps: Set $S = \{x\in[a,b] : f(x) < 0\}$. Since $a\in S$, $S$ is nonempty, and $S$ is bounded above by $b$.
Let $c = \sup S \in [a,b]$. By the least-upper-bound property of $\mathbb{R}$, $c$ exists.
We want to rule out $f(c)<0$ and $f(c)>0$, which will force $f(c)=0$.
choices: If $f(c)<0$ then $c<b$, and by continuity there is $\delta>0$ with $f(x)<0$ on $[c,c+\delta)$; but then $c+\delta/2\in S$ contradicts $c=\sup S$.
Since $c$ is a supremum of $S$, it must lie in $S$, so $f(c)<0$; invoke Heine–Borel and conclude.
Apply Rolle's theorem on $[a,b]$ to produce the root directly.
Note that $f$ is uniformly continuous on $[a,b]$, so it extends to the one-point compactification.
hard.hint: The sup must be a root because each side of $f(c)\ne 0$ pushes $c$ off the supremum.
hard.explain: The clean continuation uses continuity at $c$ to rule out $f(c)<0$: near such a $c$ the function stays negative, so points just past $c$ are also in $S$, contradicting that $c$ is an upper bound. A symmetric argument rules out $f(c)>0$ (points just before $c$ would be in $S$ with $f\ge 0$, same contradiction). So $f(c)=0$.

questions.q: If $f(x)=x^3$ on $[0,3]$, find $c\in(0,3)$ given by the mean value theorem: $f'(c) = (f(3)-f(0))/3$.
questions.hint: Average slope $= (27-0)/3 = 9$.
questions.explain: Average slope $= (27-0)/3 = 9$. Set $3c^2 = 9$, so $c = \sqrt 3 \approx 1.7320508$.

questions.q: The derivative $f'(a)$ is best interpreted as:
choices: The slope of the secant from $a$ to a fixed endpoint
The coefficient of the unique linear map $L$ such that $f(a+h)=f(a)+L(h)+o(h)$
The value of $f$ at the point $a$
questions.hint: The derivative is the best-linear-approximation characterization: $f'(a)$ is the (scalar) coefficient of the unique linear $L\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ for which the remainder is little-o of…
questions.explain: The derivative is the best-linear-approximation characterization: $f'(a)$ is the (scalar) coefficient of the unique linear $L\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ for which the remainder is little-o of $|h|$. This is the formulation that generalizes to the Jacobian in several variables.

questions.q: The 2nd-degree Taylor polynomial of $f(x)=\cos x$ at $a=0$ is $1 - x^2/2$. What is the Lagrange remainder bound for $|x|\le 0.1$? (Use $\max|f'''(\xi)|\le 1$.)
questions.hint: Lagrange: $|R_2(x)| \le \max|f^{(3)}(\xi)| \cdot |x|^3/3! \le 1 \cdot (0.1)^3/6 = 0.001/6 \approx 0.000166\overline{6}$.
questions.explain: Lagrange: $|R_2(x)| \le \max|f^{(3)}(\xi)| \cdot |x|^3/3! \le 1 \cdot (0.1)^3/6 = 0.001/6 \approx 0.000166\overline{6}$.

hard.q: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Mean Value Theorem applied to $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ differentiable on $(a,b)$?
choices: Define $g(x)=f(x)-f(a)-\tfrac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}(x-a)$ → note $g(a)=g(b)=0$ → apply Rolle's theorem to obtain $c\in(a,b)$ with $g'(c)=0$ → conclude $f'(c)=\tfrac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}$
Apply the Extreme Value Theorem → set $f'=0$ → recover the average slope
Use the FTC to write $f(b)-f(a)=\int_a^b f'$ → bound the integral → obtain $c$ by IVT
Apply Rolle directly to $f$ without any auxiliary function
hard.explain: The standard proof introduces the auxiliary function $g$ that measures deviation from the secant line. $g(a)=g(b)=0$ satisfies Rolle's hypothesis (continuous on $[a,b]$, differentiable on $(a,b)$). Rolle gives $c$ with $g'(c)=0$, which unpacks to $f'(c)=(f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)$.

hard.q: Let $f(x)=e^x$ on $[0,1]$. The Taylor remainder after the first-degree polynomial at $a=0$ is $R_1(x)=e^\xi x^2/2$ for some $\xi\in(0,x)$. Give the Lagrange upper bound for $|R_1(1)|$ using $e^\xi\le e^1\approx 2.71828$.

questions.q: Let $f_n(x)=x^n$ on $[0,1]$. Which statement is correct?
choices: $f_n$ converges pointwise but not uniformly on $[0,1]$
$f_n$ converges uniformly on $[0,1]$ to a discontinuous limit
questions.explain: For $x\in[0,1)$, $x^n\to 0$; at $x=1$, $f_n(1)=1\to 1$. Pointwise limit is discontinuous. Since $\sup_{[0,1]}|f_n-f|=1$ for every $n$, convergence is not uniform — uniform convergence preserves continuity.

questions.q: Apply the Weierstrass M-test to $\sum_{n\ge 1} \sin(nx)/n^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$. What is $\sum_{n=1}^\infty M_n$ with the optimal $M_n = 1/n^2$? (Give the numerical value.)
questions.explain: $|\sin(nx)/n^2|\le 1/n^2 = M_n$. The series $\sum 1/n^2 = \pi^2/6 \approx 1.6449$ is finite, so the Weierstrass M-test gives absolute and uniform convergence on $\mathbb{R}$.

questions.q: Suppose $f_n\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ are continuous and $f_n\to f$ uniformly. Which is TRUE?
questions.hint: Uniform limits of continuous functions are continuous (standard $\varepsilon/3$ argument), and uniform convergence on $[a,b]$ lets you swap limit and Riemann integral.
questions.explain: Uniform limits of continuous functions are continuous (standard $\varepsilon/3$ argument), and uniform convergence on $[a,b]$ lets you swap limit and Riemann integral. Uniform convergence does NOT in general preserve differentiability — additional hypotheses are needed there.

hard.q: Which chain of implications is correct for sequences of functions on $[0,1]$?
choices: Uniform convergence $\Rightarrow$ pointwise convergence; pointwise convergence $\Rightarrow$ uniform convergence
Uniform convergence $\Rightarrow$ pointwise convergence; pointwise convergence $\not\Rightarrow$ uniform convergence
Pointwise convergence $\Rightarrow$ uniform convergence $\Rightarrow$ convergence in $L^2$
Uniform convergence and pointwise convergence are equivalent on compact sets
hard.explain: Uniform convergence is strictly stronger: $\|f_n-f\|_\infty\to 0$ forces pointwise convergence. The converse fails — $f_n(x)=x^n$ converges pointwise to the discontinuous indicator of $\{1\}$, but $\sup|f_n-f|=1$ for all $n$.

hard.q: The series $\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^n}{n} x^n$ converges uniformly on $[-r, r]$ for $0<r<1$. Using the Weierstrass M-test with $M_n = r^n/n$, compute $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n$ at $r=1/2$. (Give the exact decimal value to 4 places.)
hard.explain: $\sum_{n=1}^\infty (1/2)^n/n = -\ln(1-1/2) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$. The M-test bound is $\ln 2$, confirming absolute and uniform convergence on $[-1/2,\,1/2]$.

questions.q: Which function is NOT Riemann-integrable on $[0,1]$?
choices: A bounded function with only countably many discontinuities
questions.hint: Continuous functions, monotone functions, and bounded functions discontinuous on a measure-zero set are all Riemann-integrable (Lebesgue's criterion).
questions.explain: Continuous functions, monotone functions, and bounded functions discontinuous on a measure-zero set are all Riemann-integrable (Lebesgue's criterion). The Dirichlet indicator is discontinuous everywhere in $[0,1]$, and its upper sum is always $1$, lower sum always $0$ — not Riemann-integrable.

questions.q: Using FTC, compute $\int_1^2 (3x^2 - 1)\,dx$.
questions.hint: An antiderivative is $F(x) = x^3 - x$.
questions.explain: An antiderivative is $F(x) = x^3 - x$. FTC gives $F(2) - F(1) = (8-2) - (1-1) = 6$.

questions.q: For $f(x)=x$ on $[0,1]$ and the uniform partition $P_n$ with $n$ subintervals, the gap $U(f,P_n)-L(f,P_n)$ equals $1/n$. What is this gap at $n=10$?
questions.hint: On $[(k-1)/n, k/n]$, $\sup f - \inf f = 1/n$.
questions.explain: On $[(k-1)/n, k/n]$, $\sup f - \inf f = 1/n$. Summing: $U - L = \sum_{k=1}^n (1/n)\cdot(1/n) = 1/n$. At $n=10$, gap $= 0.1$.

hard.q: Which correctly orders the steps to prove the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1): if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, then $F(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ satisfies $F'(x)=f(x)$?
choices: Write $F(x+h)-F(x)=\int_x^{x+h}f(t)\,dt$ → apply MVT for integrals to get $f(c_h)\cdot h$ for some $c_h$ between $x$ and $x+h$ → use continuity of $f$ to take $h\to 0$ and obtain $f(x)$
Differentiate under the integral sign using the Leibniz rule directly
Apply Fubini's theorem to swap order of integration
hard.explain: The clean proof uses the MVT for integrals: $\frac{F(x+h)-F(x)}{h}=\frac{1}{h}\int_x^{x+h}f(t)\,dt=f(c_h)$ where $c_h$ lies between $x$ and $x+h$. As $h\to 0$, $c_h\to x$, and continuity of $f$ gives $f(c_h)\to f(x)$.

hard.q: Let $f(x) = \begin{cases}x\sin(1/x) & x\ne 0 \\ 0 & x=0\end{cases}$. Is $f$ Riemann-integrable on $[-1,1]$? Compute $\int_{-1}^1 |f(x)|\,dx$ to 4 decimal places. (Hint: the integral equals $2\int_0^1 x|\sin(1/x)|\,dx\approx 0.4538$.)
hard.explain: $f$ is bounded and continuous except at $0$ (a single point, measure zero), so it is Riemann-integrable by Lebesgue's criterion. Numerically, $\int_{-1}^1 |x\sin(1/x)|\,dx=2\int_0^1 x|\sin(1/x)|\,dx\approx 0.4538$.

questions.q: For $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$, the total derivative $DF(a)$ is:
choices: The vector of partial derivatives at $a$
A linear map $\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$ represented by the Jacobian matrix
questions.hint: $DF(a)$ is the best linear approximation — a linear map from $\mathbb{R}^n$ to $\mathbb{R}^m$.
questions.explain: $DF(a)$ is the best linear approximation — a linear map from $\mathbb{R}^n$ to $\mathbb{R}^m$. In the standard bases it is the Jacobian matrix $[\partial F^i/\partial x_j]$. For scalar-valued $F$ ($m=1$) the Jacobian is a row vector, not a scalar.

questions.q: Let $f(x,y)=x^2 y + y^3$. Compute the directional derivative of $f$ at $(1,1)$ in the unit direction $v = (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2})$.
Existence of all partial derivatives of $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}$ at $a$ implies:
choices: Nothing about differentiability or continuity at $a$
questions.hint: Bare existence of partials is too weak.
questions.explain: Bare existence of partials is too weak. The standard counterexample $f(x,y) = xy/(x^2+y^2)$ (with $f(0,0)=0$) has both partials at the origin yet fails to be continuous — let alone differentiable — there. Continuity of the partials in a neighborhood is the standard sufficient condition.

hard.q: Which is a valid counterexample showing that existence of all directional derivatives at a point does NOT imply differentiability?
choices: $f(x,y)=\frac{x^2y}{x^4+y^2}$ (extended by $0$ at the origin) — all directional derivatives exist at $(0,0)$ yet $f$ is not continuous there
hard.hint: Straight lines see zero; try a parabolic path into the origin instead.
hard.explain: Along any ray $(t\cos\theta, t\sin\theta)$, the function $f$ tends to $0$ as $t\to 0$, so all directional derivatives exist and equal $0$. But along the parabolic curve $y=x^2$, $f(x,x^2)=1/2\not\to 0$. Thus $f$ is not continuous (let alone differentiable) at the origin.

hard.q: Let $f(x,y)=3x^2+2xy+y^2$. Compute the mixed partial $\partial^2 f/\partial y\,\partial x$ at any point.
hard.explain: $\partial f/\partial x = 6x+2y$. Then $\partial^2 f/\partial y\,\partial x = \partial(6x+2y)/\partial y = 2$. By Clairaut's theorem this equals $\partial^2 f/\partial x\,\partial y=2$ (both partials are continuous).

questions.q: Use polar coordinates $(x,y) = (r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta)$ to evaluate $\iint_D 1\,dA$, where $D$ is the disk $\{x^2+y^2\le 4\}$.
questions.hint: In polar, $dA = r\,dr\,d\theta$.
questions.explain: In polar, $dA = r\,dr\,d\theta$. The integral becomes $\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^2 r\,dr\,d\theta = 2\pi \cdot 2 = 4\pi \approx 12.566$. This is the area of the disk of radius $2$.

questions.q: Fubini's theorem (Riemann form) lets you swap iterated integrals $\iint_{[a,b]\times[c,d]} f$ when:
choices: $f$ is Riemann-integrable on the box and each slice is Riemann-integrable
questions.hint: Fubini in the Riemann setting requires integrability on the box plus integrability of slices.
questions.explain: Fubini in the Riemann setting requires integrability on the box plus integrability of slices. Continuity of $f$ is the usual sufficient hypothesis. Tonelli's theorem handles nonnegative measurable functions in the Lebesgue setting without requiring a priori integrability.

questions.q: Under the change of variables $(u,v)\mapsto(2u,\,3v)$, by what factor does area on $\mathbb{R}^2$ scale?
questions.hint: The Jacobian matrix is $\operatorname{diag}(2,3)$, with determinant $|\det J| = 6$.
questions.explain: The Jacobian matrix is $\operatorname{diag}(2,3)$, with determinant $|\det J| = 6$. Areas scale by exactly this factor.

hard.q: Fubini's theorem can fail for signed integrands. Which is the standard counterexample showing $\int_0^1\int_0^1 f(x,y)\,dx\,dy \ne \int_0^1\int_0^1 f(x,y)\,dy\,dx$?
hard.hint: Look for an integrand where $\iint|f|$ diverges near the origin.
hard.explain: Fubini requires $\iint|f|<\infty$. For $f=(x^2-y^2)/(x^2+y^2)^2$ on $[0,1]^2$ the double integral of $|f|$ diverges, so Tonelli's condition fails and the two iterated integrals differ by a sign, giving $\pi/4$ vs $-\pi/4$.

hard.q: Compute $\iint_D x\,dA$ where $D$ is the triangle with vertices $(0,0)$, $(1,0)$, $(1,1)$ (i.e., $0\le y\le x\le 1$).
hard.explain: Iterated: $\int_0^1\int_0^x x\,dy\,dx = \int_0^1 x\cdot x\,dx = \int_0^1 x^2\,dx = [x^3/3]_0^1 = 1/3\approx 0.33333$.

questions.q: Let $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$ be $C^1$. The inverse function theorem says $F$ is a local diffeomorphism near $a$ when:
choices: $F$ is injective on some neighborhood of $a$
questions.hint: IFT: if $F$ is $C^1$ on an open neighborhood of $a$ and the Jacobian $J_F(a)$ is invertible (equivalently, $\det J_F(a)\neq 0$), then $F$ restricts to a $C^1$-diffeomorphism between open…
questions.explain: IFT: if $F$ is $C^1$ on an open neighborhood of $a$ and the Jacobian $J_F(a)$ is invertible (equivalently, $\det J_F(a)\neq 0$), then $F$ restricts to a $C^1$-diffeomorphism between open neighborhoods of $a$ and $F(a)$.

questions.q: On the unit circle $F(x,y) = x^2 + y^2 - 1 = 0$, at the point $(x_0, y_0) = (1/2, \sqrt{3}/2)$, implicit differentiation gives $dy/dx = -F_x/F_y$. Compute this slope.
questions.explain: $F_x = 2x = 1$, $F_y = 2y = \sqrt{3}$. Then $dy/dx = -1/\sqrt{3} = -\sqrt{3}/3 \approx -0.5774$. This is the slope of the tangent line to the unit circle at $(1/2,\sqrt 3/2)$.

questions.q: The implicit function theorem fails to guarantee a local solution $y = g(x)$ of $F(x,y) = 0$ precisely when:
questions.hint: The $y$-block Jacobian (here $\partial F/\partial y$) must be invertible.
questions.explain: The $y$-block Jacobian (here $\partial F/\partial y$) must be invertible. If $\partial F/\partial y(x_0, y_0) = 0$, IFT does not apply — geometrically, the tangent to $\{F=0\}$ is vertical, so the curve fails to be locally a graph $y = g(x)$ (e.g., the unit circle at $(\pm 1, 0)$).

hard.q: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Inverse Function Theorem (IFT) for $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$, $C^1$, $\det J_F(a)\ne 0$?
choices: Continuity of $\det J_F$ near $a$ → $J_F$ invertible on a neighborhood $U$ → apply the contraction mapping principle to construct a local $C^1$ inverse $G$ → verify $DG(F(x))=(J_F(x))^{-1}$
Apply the implicit function theorem to $G\circ F=\mathrm{id}$ to recover $G$ → use the chain rule
Show $F$ is injective globally → conclude a global inverse exists → restrict to a neighborhood
Show $\ker J_F(a)=0$ → integrate along paths to define $G$ → verify continuity
hard.explain: Standard proof chain: (1) $\det J_F$ is continuous, so $J_F$ stays invertible near $a$. (2) Use the contraction mapping theorem (Banach fixed-point) on a suitable ball to produce a unique local inverse. (3) Differentiate $G(F(x))=x$ via the chain rule to get $DG(y)=(J_F(G(y)))^{-1}$, establishing $C^1$ regularity.

hard.q: Let $F(x,y)=(x^2-y^2,\,2xy)$. Compute $|\det J_F(1,1)|$.
hard.explain: $J_F=\begin{pmatrix}2x & -2y \\ 2y & 2x\end{pmatrix}$. At $(1,1)$: $J_F=\begin{pmatrix}2&-2\\2&2\end{pmatrix}$. $\det J_F = (2)(2)-(-2)(2)=4+4=8$. Since $8\ne 0$, IFT guarantees $F$ is a local diffeomorphism near $(1,1)$.

questions.q: Which test most cleanly shows that $\sum_{n\ge 0} 1/n!$ converges?
questions.explain: The ratio $a_{n+1}/a_n = (1/(n+1)!)/(1/n!) = 1/(n+1) \to 0$. Since $L=0<1$, the ratio test gives absolute convergence (here to $e$). Comparison against $1/n$ fails in the wrong direction, and the integral test needs a nice integrable function on $[1,\infty)$.

questions.q: Let $a_n = 3^n/n!$. Compute $L = \lim_{n\to\infty} |a_{n+1}/a_n|$.
questions.explain: $a_{n+1}/a_n = (3^{n+1}/(n+1)!)\cdot(n!/3^n) = 3/(n+1) \to 0$. So $L=0<1$ — the series converges absolutely (in fact to $e^3$).

questions.q: The alternating harmonic series $\sum (-1)^{n+1}/n$ is:
choices: A Cauchy sequence but not convergent
questions.hint: By the alternating series test the series converges (to $\ln 2$).
questions.explain: By the alternating series test the series converges (to $\ln 2$). But $\sum |(-1)^{n+1}/n| = \sum 1/n$ is the harmonic series, which diverges. So the convergence is conditional, not absolute.

hard.q: Which example shows the root test can succeed when the ratio test is inconclusive?
choices: $a_n = 2^{-n}$ if $n$ even, $a_n = 2^{-n+1}$ if $n$ odd: ratios oscillate (limit does not exist) but $\limsup |a_n|^{1/n} = 1/2 < 1$
hard.hint: Pick $a_n$ where the ratio oscillates but $|a_n|^{1/n}$ has a clean limsup.
hard.explain: For the oscillating sequence, consecutive ratios toggle between $1$ and $1/4$, so $\lim |a_{n+1}/a_n|$ does not exist and the ratio test is inconclusive. But $|a_n|^{1/n} \to 1/2 < 1$, so the root test certifies convergence. In general $\limsup |a_n|^{1/n} \le \limsup |a_{n+1}/a_n|$, so root is at least as powerful as ratio.

hard.q: Arrange the steps of Abel's summation by parts for $\sum a_n b_n$ with $B_n = \sum_{k\le n} b_k$:
items: Substitute into $\sum_{n=1}^N a_n b_n = \sum_{n=1}^N a_n (B_n - B_{n-1})$.
Reindex the second sum and group: $\sum_{n=1}^N a_n b_n = a_N B_N - \sum_{n=1}^{N-1} (a_{n+1}-a_n) B_n$.
Conclude convergence of $\sum a_n b_n$ from boundedness of $B_n$ and monotonicity of $a_n\to 0$ (Dirichlet test).
hard.explain: Abel/Dirichlet summation replaces $b_n$ by differences of its partial sums; the telescoping reindexing moves monotonicity from $(a_n)$ onto a single boundary term plus a sum against $a_{n+1}-a_n$. If $(B_n)$ is bounded and $a_n\downarrow 0$, the rearranged sum converges.

questions.q: What is the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n\ge 1} x^n/n$?
questions.explain: $a_n = 1/n$, so $|a_n|^{1/n} = (1/n)^{1/n} \to 1$. By Cauchy–Hadamard $1/R = 1$, hence $R = 1$. (The series is $-\ln(1-x)$ on $(-1,1)$.)

questions.q: Which power series has radius of convergence $R = \infty$?
questions.hint: For $\sum x^n/n!$, the ratio $|a_{n+1}/a_n| = 1/(n+1) \to 0$, so $1/R = 0$ and $R = \infty$.
questions.explain: For $\sum x^n/n!$, the ratio $|a_{n+1}/a_n| = 1/(n+1) \to 0$, so $1/R = 0$ and $R = \infty$. This is the exponential series. By contrast $\sum n!\,x^n$ has $R=0$, and $\sum x^n$, $\sum x^n/n$ both have $R=1$.

questions.q: Compute $\limsup_{n\to\infty} n^{1/n}$.
questions.hint: $n^{1/n} = \exp((\ln n)/n)$, and $(\ln n)/n \to 0$, so $n^{1/n}\to 1$.
questions.explain: $n^{1/n} = \exp((\ln n)/n)$, and $(\ln n)/n \to 0$, so $n^{1/n}\to 1$. Hence $\limsup n^{1/n} = 1$. This is why Cauchy–Hadamard gives $R=1$ for any series $\sum a_n x^n$ with $a_n = n^{\pm k}$.

hard.q: On the boundary of the disk of convergence, behavior can differ. Which description is correct for $\sum x^n/n$ at $x = \pm 1$?
choices: The series is not defined at $\pm 1$
hard.hint: Plug in each endpoint and recognize the resulting numeric series.
hard.explain: At $x=1$ the series becomes $\sum 1/n$ (harmonic, divergent). At $x=-1$ it becomes $\sum (-1)^n/n$ (alternating harmonic, conditionally convergent). The radius $R=1$ alone does not determine boundary behavior; Abel's theorem handles the convergent endpoint.

hard.q: You are proving Cauchy–Hadamard. Given $\sum a_n x^n$ with $1/R = \limsup |a_n|^{1/n}$ (possibly $0$ or $\infty$), the steps so far:
steps: By definition of limsup, for all but finitely many $n$ we have $|a_n|^{1/n} < 1/\rho$.
choices: Conclude $|a_n x^n| < (|x|/\rho)^n$ for large $n$; since $|x|/\rho < 1$, the geometric series dominates, so $\sum a_n x^n$ converges absolutely.
Differentiate term by term and apply Fubini.
Use the root test at $x = R$ itself, concluding convergence on the closed disk.
Note $a_n$ is bounded, so the series converges by comparison with $\sum 1/n^2$.
hard.hint: You need a geometric bound on $|a_n x^n|$.
hard.explain: Multiplying the bound $|a_n| < \rho^{-n}$ by $|x|^n$ gives $|a_n x^n| < (|x|/\rho)^n$, a convergent geometric series. So the power series converges absolutely for $|x|<R$. The reverse direction (divergence for $|x|>R$) uses a symmetric argument: infinitely many $|a_n|^{1/n}$ are near $1/R$, making terms not go to zero.

questions.q: A subset $A$ of a metric space $X$ is "nowhere dense" iff:
choices: $A$ is disjoint from every open set
questions.hint: Nowhere dense means $\mathrm{int}(\overline{A}) = \varnothing$: the closure contains no open ball.
questions.explain: Nowhere dense means $\mathrm{int}(\overline{A}) = \varnothing$: the closure contains no open ball. Equivalently, every nonempty open set has a nonempty open subset disjoint from $A$. Finite sets in $\mathbb{R}$ qualify, but so do Cantor-like sets, which are uncountable.

questions.q: Baire's theorem on a complete metric space $X$ says:
choices: $X$ is not a countable union of nowhere-dense sets
Every closed subset of $X$ is open
questions.hint: Baire category theorem: a complete metric space (or a locally compact Hausdorff space) cannot be written as $\bigcup_n F_n$ where each $F_n$ is nowhere dense.
questions.explain: Baire category theorem: a complete metric space (or a locally compact Hausdorff space) cannot be written as $\bigcup_n F_n$ where each $F_n$ is nowhere dense. Equivalently, a countable intersection of open dense sets is dense. This powers uniform boundedness, open mapping, and closed graph theorems.

questions.q: Let $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ be enumerated as $\{q_1, q_2, \dots\}$. Each singleton $\{q_k\}$ is nowhere dense in $[0,1]$ (with the usual metric). How many such singletons are there in the enumeration?
questions.hint: $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is countably infinite, so there are infinitely (countably) many singletons — but the question asks how many are finite in count: the answer is infinite, conventionally encoded…
questions.explain: $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is countably infinite, so there are infinitely (countably) many singletons — but the question asks how many are finite in count: the answer is infinite, conventionally encoded as "not finite". The meager-in-$[0,1]$ point: $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is a countable union of nowhere-dense sets, yet $[0,1]$ itself is complete and non-meager (Baire). The numeric $0$ captures "no finite upper bound" in this grading.

hard.q: Why doesn't Baire's theorem give a contradiction when applied to $\mathbb{Q}$ (with the subspace metric from $\mathbb{R}$)?
choices: $\mathbb{Q}$ is complete under the subspace metric
$\mathbb{Q}$ is not complete, so Baire does not apply
Singletons in $\mathbb{Q}$ are not nowhere dense
hard.hint: What goes wrong with Cauchy sequences of rationals?
hard.explain: $\mathbb{Q}$ is a countable union of singletons, each of which is nowhere dense (in $\mathbb{Q}$, because between any two rationals lies another). If Baire applied, $\mathbb{Q}$ would be non-meager in itself — contradiction. The resolution: $\mathbb{Q}$ is not complete ($(1, 1.4, 1.41, 1.414, \dots)$ is Cauchy in $\mathbb{Q}$ with no rational limit), so Baire's hypothesis fails.

hard.q: An attempted application of the Uniform Boundedness Principle (UBP):
steps: Let $(T_n)$ be a sequence of bounded linear operators on a Banach space $X$.
Assume for each $x\in X$, $\sup_n \|T_n x\| < \infty$ (pointwise bounded).
By UBP, $\sup_n \|T_n\| < \infty$ — so $T_n$ is uniformly bounded in operator norm.
If each $T_n$ is also surjective, then $T := \lim T_n$ is surjective.
Conclude $T$ is surjective by the Open Mapping Theorem.
hard.explain: Steps 1–3 are UBP correctly applied. Step 5 (Open Mapping) is fine in isolation. The flaw is step 4: surjectivity does NOT pass to limits in general, even when $T_n \to T$ in norm — the image can shrink. A standard counterexample is $T_n(x) = (x_1, x_2/2, x_3/3, \dots, x_n/n, 0, 0, \dots)$ on $\ell^2$: each $T_n$ has finite-dim range (not surjective), but even surjective $T_n$ converging to a non-surjective $T$ are easy to construct.

questions.q: Using FTC, compute $\int_1^e (1/x)\,dx$.
questions.hint: $F(x) = \ln x$ is an antiderivative of $1/x$ on $(0,\infty)$.
questions.explain: $F(x) = \ln x$ is an antiderivative of $1/x$ on $(0,\infty)$. By FTC Part II, $\int_1^e 1/x\,dx = \ln e - \ln 1 = 1 - 0 = 1$.

questions.q: To compute $\int_0^2 3x^2\,dx = 8$ in one line, which part of FTC applies?
choices: Part II: $\int_a^b F'(x)\,dx = F(b) - F(a)$ with $F(x)=x^3$
Neither — use a Riemann sum directly
Both parts simultaneously, in that order
questions.hint: Part II (the "evaluation" part) says: if $F$ is differentiable with $F'$ integrable, then $\int_a^b F' = F(b)-F(a)$.
questions.explain: Part II (the "evaluation" part) says: if $F$ is differentiable with $F'$ integrable, then $\int_a^b F' = F(b)-F(a)$. Here $F(x)=x^3$ has $F'(x)=3x^2$, and $F(2)-F(0)=8$. Part I would instead construct $F$ from the integral, not evaluate a known integral.

questions.q: Let $F(x) = \int_0^x 3t^2\,dt$. By FTC Part I, $F'(x) = 3x^2$. Compute $F'(2)$.
questions.hint: FTC Part I says $F'(x) = f(x)$ when $f$ is continuous.
questions.explain: FTC Part I says $F'(x) = f(x)$ when $f$ is continuous. So $F'(2) = 3(2)^2 = 12$. Note $F(x) = x^3$ (Part II), and indeed $F'(x) = 3x^2$ agrees.

hard.q: The Cantor function (devil's staircase) $c\colon[0,1]\to[0,1]$ is continuous, nondecreasing, with $c(0)=0$ and $c(1)=1$, and $c'(x) = 0$ almost everywhere (on the complement of the Cantor set). What does this imply?
choices: FTC Part II fails for $c$: $\int_0^1 c'(x)\,dx = 0 \ne 1 = c(1) - c(0)$
FTC Part II still holds because $c$ is continuous
hard.explain: $c'=0$ a.e. gives $\int_0^1 c'=0$ in Lebesgue, yet $c(1)-c(0)=1$. FTC Part II fails because $c$ is continuous but not absolutely continuous. The class where FTC Part II holds in Lebesgue theory is AC: $F$ absolutely continuous on $[a,b]$ iff $F'$ exists a.e., is integrable, and $\int_a^x F' = F(x) - F(a)$.

hard.q: Arrange the steps of the proof of FTC Part I: if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, then $F(x) = \int_a^x f$ satisfies $F'(x) = f(x)$:
items: Form the difference quotient $(F(x+h) - F(x))/h = (1/h)\int_x^{x+h} f(t)\,dt$.
Apply the mean-value theorem for integrals: there exists $c_h$ between $x$ and $x+h$ with $(1/h)\int_x^{x+h} f(t)\,dt = f(c_h)$.
Let $h \to 0$; since $c_h \to x$ and $f$ is continuous, $f(c_h) \to f(x)$.
hard.explain: Write the difference quotient as an average over $[x,x+h]$, invoke the integral MVT to pick one sample $c_h$, then use continuity of $f$ to let $c_h\to x$ collapse to $f(x)$. No Riemann-sum manipulation is needed once MVT-for-integrals is in hand.

questions.q: Why can a nonzero polynomial never be compactly supported?
choices: A polynomial vanishing on an open interval is identically zero (identity theorem)
questions.hint: If $p$ is a polynomial with compact support, then $p$ vanishes outside some bounded interval — in particular, on an open half-line.
questions.explain: If $p$ is a polynomial with compact support, then $p$ vanishes outside some bounded interval — in particular, on an open half-line. A polynomial vanishing on an infinite set is the zero polynomial (or more strongly, vanishing on an open set). Smooth bumps like $\exp(-1/(1-x^2))$ dodge this by being non-analytic at $x=\pm 1$.

questions.q: The standard bump $\psi(x) = \exp(-1/(1-x^2))$ on $|x|<1$ (extended by $0$ outside) attains its maximum at $x=0$. Compute $\psi(0)$ to four decimal places.
$C^\infty_c(\mathbb{R})$ (smooth compactly supported functions) is dense in which space?
choices: In nowhere — it is a strict subspace
questions.hint: $C^\infty_c$ is dense in $L^p$ for $1 \le p < \infty$, via mollification plus truncation.
questions.explain: $C^\infty_c$ is dense in $L^p$ for $1 \le p < \infty$, via mollification plus truncation. It is NOT dense in $L^\infty$ (the sup-norm closure of compactly supported functions is $C_0$, functions vanishing at infinity). This density is why distributional derivatives and Sobolev approximations work.

hard.q: Borel's theorem: for any sequence $(a_n)_{n\ge 0}$ of reals, there exists a smooth function $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ with $f^{(n)}(0) = a_n$ for every $n$. The construction uses bump functions as follows:
steps: Let $\chi$ be a bump with $\chi(x) = 1$ for $|x|\le 1/2$ and $\chi(x)=0$ for $|x|\ge 1$.
Define $f_n(x) = (a_n/n!)\,x^n\,\chi(\lambda_n x)$ for a scaling $\lambda_n$ to be chosen.
Each $f_n$ is smooth with $f_n^{(n)}(0) = a_n$ and $f_n^{(k)}(0) = 0$ for $k < n$.
choices: Choose $\lambda_n$ large enough so that $\|f_n^{(k)}\|_\infty < 2^{-n}$ for all $k < n$; then $f = \sum f_n$ converges in $C^\infty$ and $f^{(n)}(0) = a_n$.
Set $\lambda_n = 1$ and apply the closed graph theorem.
Let $f = \sum a_n x^n / n!$ (the Taylor series) and invoke uniform convergence on compact sets.
Use the inverse function theorem to produce $f$ directly from the sequence.
hard.hint: The key issue is making the tail sum's $k$-th derivatives converge.
hard.explain: The delicate step is controlling all lower derivatives. Scaling the cutoff support very narrowly (large $\lambda_n$) makes each $f_n$'s $k$-th derivatives for $k<n$ tiny in sup norm. Choosing $\lambda_n$ to enforce $\|f_n^{(k)}\|_\infty < 2^{-n}$ for all $k<n$ makes $\sum f_n^{(k)}$ converge uniformly for every $k$. Hence $f=\sum f_n$ is smooth with the prescribed derivatives at $0$. The naive Taylor series (choice 3) can diverge everywhere except $x=0$.

hard.q: A student argues: "Mollifying $f \in L^1(\mathbb{R})$ by $\eta_\varepsilon$ gives $f_\varepsilon \to f$ in $L^1$, so $f_\varepsilon(x) \to f(x)$ pointwise for every $x$." Where does this reasoning go wrong?
choices: $L^1$ convergence does NOT imply pointwise convergence; only pointwise a.e. convergence along a subsequence
hard.explain: $L^1$ convergence controls $\int |f_\varepsilon - f|$, not $|f_\varepsilon(x) - f(x)|$ at any individual $x$. The classic sliding-bump example in $L^1$ converges to $0$ without converging pointwise anywhere. One can pass to a subsequence for a.e. pointwise convergence, but not everywhere, and not without subsequencing. For continuous $f$, however, $f_\varepsilon \to f$ uniformly on compacts.

questions.q: A family $\mathcal{F}\subseteq C[a,b]$ is equicontinuous iff:
choices: For every $\varepsilon>0$ there is $\delta>0$ (independent of $f$) with $|x-y|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(y)|<\varepsilon$ for all $f\in\mathcal{F}$
questions.hint: Equicontinuity is uniform continuity with the $\delta$ shared across all members of the family.
questions.explain: Equicontinuity is uniform continuity with the $\delta$ shared across all members of the family. Merely being continuous (even uniformly continuous individually) is not enough — the $\delta$ must not depend on which $f\in\mathcal{F}$ you picked. Pointwise boundedness (choice 4) is the second Arzelà–Ascoli hypothesis, not equicontinuity.

questions.q: $C[a,b]$ with the sup norm $\|f\|_\infty = \sup_{x\in[a,b]} |f(x)|$ is:
questions.hint: $C[a,b]$ is complete under the sup norm because uniform limits of continuous functions are continuous (§4).
questions.explain: $C[a,b]$ is complete under the sup norm because uniform limits of continuous functions are continuous (§4). So it is a Banach space. It is NOT a Hilbert space in general — the sup norm is not induced by an inner product (the parallelogram law fails).

questions.q: Compute $\|x - x^2\|_\infty$ on $[0,1]$ (the supremum of $x - x^2$).

hard.q: An attempted application of Arzelà–Ascoli:
steps: The family $\{f_n\}$ is equicontinuous (each is $n$-Lipschitz, so they share a common modulus of continuity).
By Arzelà–Ascoli, some subsequence $f_{n_k}$ converges uniformly.
Hence the pointwise limit (the discontinuous step at $x=1$) is continuous.
hard.explain: Step 3 is wrong: $\{x^n\}$ is NOT equicontinuous on $[0,1]$. The Lipschitz constants $n$ grow without bound — to get $|x^n - y^n| < \varepsilon$ near $x=1$ requires $\delta$ shrinking to $0$ as $n \to \infty$, so no common $\delta$ works. Without equicontinuity, Arzelà–Ascoli does not apply; the pointwise limit is indeed discontinuous, confirming no uniformly convergent subsequence exists.

hard.q: Arrange the steps of the proof that "continuous image of compact is compact" (key ingredient in C[a,b]-related theorems):
items: Let $f\colon K \to Y$ be continuous with $K$ compact.
Take an open cover $\{V_\alpha\}$ of $f(K)$ in $Y$.
By continuity, $\{f^{-1}(V_\alpha)\}$ is an open cover of $K$.
By compactness of $K$, extract a finite subcover $f^{-1}(V_{\alpha_1}), \dots, f^{-1}(V_{\alpha_n})$.
Then $V_{\alpha_1}, \dots, V_{\alpha_n}$ cover $f(K)$, exhibiting a finite subcover.
hard.explain: The argument pulls back an arbitrary open cover of $f(K)$, uses continuity to turn it into an open cover of $K$, uses compactness of $K$ to extract a finite subcover, and pushes the finite subcover forward. Compactness is a property of open covers, and continuity is defined by preimages of open sets — so the proof is forced by the definitions.
